Lithuania, officially known as the Republic of Lithuania, is located in Eastern Europe.
It is bordered by Latvia to the north, Belarus to the east and south, Poland to the south, and Russia to the southwest.
Lithuania covers an area of 65,300 km² and has an estimated population of approximately 2.9 million (2025 estimate).
The capital and largest city is Vilnius; other major cities include Kaunas and Klaipeda.
The country has a rich historical timeline, with key dates including the establishment of the state in 1009 and its independence declared on 16 February 1918.
Lithuania was unified in the 1230s, leading to the formation of the state on July 6, 1253.
In 1386, Lithuania entered into a union with Poland, which later formed the Commonwealth of Poland-Lithuania by 1569.
The Commonwealth, one of the largest states in Europe, existed until its partition between 1772 and 1795.
Lithuania regained independence in 1918, and after a brief Soviet occupation, it became the first Soviet republic to declare independence on March 11, 1990.
Lithuania is a member of various international organizations and has historical roots with the name deriving from the river Lietuva, linked to its early warrior class.
Lithuania's history dates back to settlements about 10,000 years ago, with early inhabitants being traveling hunters.
Inhabitants transitioned to local hunting and fishing in the warmer climate of the 8th millennium BC.
Baltic tribes mixed with incoming populations around the 3rd – 2nd millennium BC but maintained distinct cultural identities.
The Kingdom of Lithuania was established in the 13th century, with Mindaugas being crowned the first king in 1253.
The territory faced invasions and political fragmentation but achieved reunification under Grand Duke Traidenis in the late 13th century.
Late 13th century: dynasty members began ruling Lithuania and expanding its territory to ~650,000 km2 by incorporating various principalities.
In 1385, Lithuania formed a union with Poland through the Union of Krewo, leading to the ruling Gediminids dynasty controlling multiple regions.
The decisive Polish–Lithuanian victory at the Battle of Grunwald in 1410 ended German attacks on Lithuania, leading to the Treaty of Torun in 1422.
In 1569, the Union of Lublin created the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, ensuring Lithuania's separate status with its own governance while sharing a monarch.

The mid-17th century saw Lithuania suffer military losses, with annexation by Russia and temporary independence from Poland, culminating in a revolt that reinstated it as part of the Commonwealth by 1657.
The Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth was divided three times by neighboring countries, leading to its dissolution in 1795 after a failed uprising.
Lithuania was annexed and subsequently ruled under stricter Russian policies, resulting in Russification efforts.
Failed attempts to restore independence occurred during uprisings in 1830-1831 and 1863-1864, both met with Russian military success and increased oppression.
S. Daukantas promoted Lithuanian cultural revival by emphasizing the country's pre-Commonwealth traditions and writing a history of Lithuania in Lithuanian.
Historical narratives were shaped by Polish and Lithuanian scholars, highlighting the rich history of Lithuania prior to its annexation.
Lithuanians resisted Russification through secret publishing and homeschooling.
The 1905 conference aimed for the restoration of Lithuanian sovereignty and unity.
Lithuania declared independence in 1918 after a brief monarchy.
Lithuania faced occupations and territorial conflicts with Poland and Soviet forces during the early 20th century.
A movement in the late 1980s led to the eventual restoration of Lithuania's independence.
Lithuania declared independence from the USSR on 11 March 1990, becoming the first Soviet-occupied state to do so.
A Soviet blockade lasted 74 days following Lithuania's declaration, causing significant shortages, but Lithuania did not retract its independence.
In January 1991, a coup attempt by Soviet forces was met with public resistance, resulting in 14 deaths and injuries to hundreds.
Lithuania adopted its current constitution on 25 October 1992 and elected its first post-independence president on 14 February 1993.
Lithuania joined NATO in March 2004, adopted the euro in January 2015, and declared a state of emergency in response to the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Lithuania located in the Baltic region with an area of 65,300 km².
Characterized by a sandy coastline of 99 km, with only 38 km facing the open sea.
Geographical features include glacial landscapes, lowlands, highlands, and mixed forest covering over 33% of the country.
Temperate climate classified as Dfb, with average temperatures ranging from -6°C in January to 17°C in July in Vilnius.
Extreme winter temperatures can drop to -43°C in eastern Lithuania.
Average annual precipitation: 800 mm on the coast, 900 mm in Samogitia highlands, 600 mm in eastern Lithuania.
Snow falls annually from October to April; growing season varies from 202 days in the west to 169 days in the east.
Historical temperature data show warming trends, with notable periods of coolness; a notable drought occurred in 2002.
Environmental Protection Act adopted in 1992, aiming for 20% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2020 and 40% by 2030.
Lithuania features thousands of lakes, dense forests, and fertile fields, with a commitment to renewable energy and biodiversity conservation.
Forests cover one-third of Lithuania's territory, crucial for timber industry (11% of industrial production).
Lithuania has lost 70% of wetlands due to drainage and peat extraction, impacting natural ecosystems.
Approximately 54% of Lithuania is agricultural land, with habitat deterioration from expanding crop areas.
Wildlife populations are recovering, with 250,000 larger wild animals; the most common is the deer (120,000).
Lithuania has a diverse ecosystem, including various endangered species and 29,000 rivers with significant ecological changes.
Lithuania restored its independence on March 11, 1990, and maintains strong democratic traditions.
The first independent general elections were held on October 25, 1992, with intense debates concerning the constitution and the role of the president.
The President of Lithuania is directly elected for a five-year term, and oversees foreign affairs and national security.
The Seimas (Lithuanian parliament) has 141 members elected to four-year terms, with a party needing at least 5% of the national vote to gain seats.
Lithuania was among the first countries to grant women the right to vote, allowing them to participate in elections from 1919.
Lithuania has a fragmented multi-party system with coalition governments and elections every four years on the second Sunday of October.
Candidates for the parliament must be at least 21 years old, reside in Lithuania, and not be under any court-imposed sentence.
In November 2024, a coalition agreement involving Social Democrats confirmed a new prime minister after gaining 52 seats in parliament.
The President, elected for a five-year term with a maximum of two terms, must be at least 40 years old and reside in Lithuania for at least three years.
Municipal councils are elected every four years, with the number of members ranging from 15 to 51 based on the municipality size; in 2023, 1,498 members were elected.
Lithuania has 11 allocated seats in the EU as of 2024.
Elections are open to Lithuanian citizens and EU citizens living in Lithuania, aged 18 or older on election day.
Candidates must be at least 21 years old, a citizen of Lithuania or another EU country, and may not run in more than one country.
Historical codification of Lithuanian laws began in 1468 with several important statutes in the 16th century.
The current legal framework was established in 1992, modifying previous Soviet laws, with an emphasis on formality and rationalization.
Lithuania's legal system has been integrated with the EU since May 1, 2004.
Crime rates in Lithuania have been declining, with police forces handling law enforcement responsibilities.
In 2017, there were 63,846 registered crimes, with significant reductions in serious crimes compared to previous years.
Corruption is perceived as widespread among Lithuanians, with 95% acknowledging it as a major issue in society.
Lithuania has the highest number of prison inmates in the EU, attributed to high repression levels rather than high crime rates.
Lithuania's administrative division system was established in 1994 and modified in 2000 to meet EU requirements.
The country is divided into counties, municipalities, and further into over 500 elderships.
Municipalities are the main administrative units, with elected councils and directly elected mayors since 2015.
Elderships provide local public services and are most active in the social sector, though they are perceived by some as lacking power.
The election cycle for municipality councils is every four years, previously three years.
Lithuania became a UN member on September 18, 1991, and has diplomatic relations with 149 countries.
Joined NATO on May 31, 2001, and the EU on July 5, 2018, while seeking further Western alliances.
Hosts regional cooperation initiatives with Nordic and Baltic countries through formats like NB6.
Participated in the CBSS, established in 1992, to promote integration and regional contacts.
Active in international organizations, hosting events like the Ministerial Council Meeting in 2011.
Lithuania cooperates with Nordic and Baltic countries in the NORDPLUS education program.
The Baltic Development Forum (BDF) is a nonprofit organization that promotes collaboration in the Baltic Sea region.
Lithuania maintains warm relations with Georgia, supporting its EU and NATO aspirations, especially during the 2008 conflict with Russia.
From 2004-2009, Lithuania was involved in the EU-led Commission and in 2013, became the first Baltic country elected to the UN Security Council.
Lithuania has condemned Russian actions in Ukraine, earning admiration, and faced increased diplomatic tensions with China over imports and intelligence activities.
The Lithuanian Armed Forces include active personnel of approximately 20,000 and have units like Logistics Command and Military Police.
Compulsory military service ended in 2008 but was reinstated in 2015, and Lithuania contributes to international operations including missions in Ukraine.
Lithuania became a NATO member in 2004 and has participated in various international operations, including ISAF in Afghanistan since 2005.
The National Defence Policy of Lithuania focuses on preserving the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the state, as well as contributing to NATO and EU missions.
The Ministry of National Defence oversees combat forces and intelligence, with the State Border Guard responsible for border protection and customs duties.
Lithuania exceeded NATO's defense spending guideline of 2% in 2019, increasing its funding after previously lagging behind NATO allies.
On April 22, 2022, Lithuania's president called for increased NATO troop deployments in response to security concerns along Europe's eastern flank.
Lithuania has an open economy classified as a high-income economy, with services, industry, and agriculture as its main sectors.
Major exports include agricultural products (18%), chemical products and plastics (18%), and machinery and appliances (16%), with over half going to 7 countries including Russia, Latvia, and Poland.
Lithuania experienced rapid GDP growth until 2009, with a significant contraction of 15% that year due to the global financial crisis, but financial conditions are currently supportive of growth.
Over 95% of FDI in Lithuania comes from EU countries, primarily from Sweden, the largest investor with 20-30% of total FDI.
In 2017, Lithuania experienced a significant increase in FDI, becoming the third-best in Europe after the US and Germany in terms of average job value.
One in five Lithuanians emigrated between 2004 and 2016, mainly due to low income, leading to a labor market shortage and increased salaries.
By 2022, Lithuanian wealth per adult was $32,000, and the average monthly gross salary reached €2,000 in Q2 2023.
Lithuania has one of the lowest corporate tax rates in the EU at 15% and is a leading country in e-money and cryptocurrency regulation in Europe.
Agriculture in Lithuania has been a key occupation since ancient times and transitioned significantly after EU accession in 2004.
In 2016, Lithuania's agricultural production was worth € billion with key products including cereals and legumes, contributing to 19% of all exports.
Organic farming is on the rise, with 2,539 registered farms cultivating 225,542 hectares; significant proportions dedicated to cereals and grasses.
The establishment of Vilnius University in 1579 spurred scientific and academic development, attracting notable scientists and innovators.
Key contributions in agriculture and science include expertise in artillery, botany, and improving peasant welfare.
World wars hindered Lithuanian science and academia, yet many scholars achieved success abroad.
Flagship fields in Lithuanian science include biotech and high-tech industry, with notable companies like Šviesos konversija dominating laser technology.
Lithuania has launched three satellites and has a growing collaboration with CERN, hosting research incubators in Vilnius and Kaunas.
The biotech and life sciences sector saw a 22% annual growth from 2011 to 2016, with numerous academic and research institutions contributing.
The Valley development program began in 2008 to enhance research infrastructure and foster cooperation between business and science in five key areas.
Lithuania ranks 15th among EU countries in tourism and was 35th globally in 2024.
In 2023, Lithuania attracted millions of foreign tourists, with the most visitors from neighboring countries.
Domestic tourism is increasing, with approximately 1000 attractions and popular cities including Vilnius, Kaunas, and Klaipėda.
Lithuania has a well-developed communications infrastructure, with 97% LTE coverage and a growing number of broadband users.
The Rural Areas Broadband Network project aims to improve internet access in rural regions, with 72% of households having internet access by 2021.
Lithuania has the highest FTTH penetration rate in Europe as of September 2016.
The Lithuanian railway network includes 1,762 km of railway, with 122 km electrified, but is incompatible with European standards.
Over half of all inland freight in Lithuania is transported by rail, with a significant portion of commercial truck traffic being international.
Lithuania has extensive motorways and is recognized for its transport sector, which is the third largest in the economy.
The only commercial cargo port in Lithuania, Klaipeda, handled 2011 million tons in cargo, and a project is underway to improve cargo shipping on the Nemunas river.
Vilnius Airport is the largest in Lithuania and the 91st busiest in Europe, serving millions of passengers in 2016.
Lithuania's energy strategy focuses on diversification of energy imports, aiming for long-term independence as outlined in the 2012 National Energy Independence strategy.
Lithuania shifted from being an electricity exporter to an importer after closing nuclear units in 2004 and 2009, with ongoing proposals for new power plants.
As of 2015, 66% of Lithuania's electricity is imported, with a notable reliance on renewable sources for 2016's consumption.
The Klaipėda LNG terminal, operational since 2014, enhances Lithuania's natural gas independence and has sufficient capacity for regional demand.
Lithuania's population is genetically homogenous, closely related to other Northern and Eastern European groups.
As of 2021, the population demographics show a median age of 44 years with a detailed age structure, including significant populations in the 0-14 and 15-64 year categories.
The total fertility rate in Lithuania was lower than the replacement level in 2021, with an average age for first childbirth for women.
Lithuania features a population of about 2.8 million in 2024, predominantly ethnic Lithuanians, making up five-sixths of the population.
Major ethnic minorities include Poles, Russians, and Belarusians, while Lithuanian is the official language.
Lithuania has significant minority populations, including Poles, Russians, Tatars, and Karaites, with high concentration in southeast regions.
Lithuanian is the official language; English and Russian are widely spoken as foreign languages with education in minority languages supported by state laws.
The Jewish population in Lithuania peaked at 250,000 in 1941 but is now very small.
Urbanization has increased since the 1990s, with two-thirds of the population living in urban areas as of 2021.
Vilnius is recognized as a top mid-sized European city for business development and investment, improving its rankings in recent years.
Lithuania's largest city in 2025 is Vilnius, with a population of 607,404.
Healthcare in Lithuania is primarily state-funded, co-existing with a significant private sector.
Lithuania's healthcare system ranked 27th in Europe in terms of waiting times and overall results as of 2016.
The country has the highest suicide rate in the EU, although it has been decreasing since the 1990s; various factors contribute to this issue.
By 2000, most healthcare institutions in Lithuania were non-profit, with various public health facilities managed by the Ministry of Health.
Lithuania has 5 Territorial Health Insurance Funds providing health coverage, with 9% income contributions from the economically active.
Emergency medical services are free for all residents, with secondary and tertiary care typically requiring a referral.
Catholicism is the predominant religion in Lithuania, with historical roots dating back to 1387, having faced persecution in the past.
Protestantism and Hinduism are present, with Hinduism being a more recent development since the 1990s.
The historical Jewish community in Lithuania suffered greatly during the Holocaust, with a steep decline from 220,000 to about 4,000 by 2009.
Romuva is a revived pagan faith in Lithuania that emphasizes nature and has increased its followers from 1,270 to 5,118 from 2001 to 2011.
Lithuania mandates ten years of free public education ending at age 16, and guarantees free higher education for qualifying students.
The literacy rate in Lithuania for those aged 15 and older is 100%, with higher school attendance rates than the EU average.
Lithuania ranks among the top five countries in the world for postsecondary education attainment, especially in STEM and business fields.
The Lithuanian education system faces challenges such as insufficient funding, low teacher salaries, and a decreasing population of students.
As of 2008, Lithuania had 15 public and 6 private universities, along with 16 public and 11 private colleges.
The largest university in Lithuania and the largest technical university in the Baltic States has undergone changes, including mergers to adapt to decreasing high-school student numbers.
Lithuanian is the official state language and has approximately 3 million native speakers in Lithuania and about 1 million abroad.
Lithuanian is a Baltic language, closely related to Latvian, and is written in an adapted Latin script.
Two main dialects are spoken: Aukštaitian and Samogitian, with significant differences in vocabulary and pronunciation.
The first Lithuanian grammar book, *Grammatica Litvanica*, was published in Latin in 1653 by Daniel Klein.
Daniel Klein's work was crucial for standardizing Lithuanian literature and introduced the letter *ū* into the language.
Significant Lithuanian literature from the Middle Ages was written in Latin, including the works of Lithuania's kings and other historical texts.
Early authors included those like Mikołaj Radwan and Petrus Roysius, who explored various themes such as national customs and landscape.
Seventeenth-century scholars continued to contribute to Lithuanian literature in Latin, focusing on theology, history, and other subjects.
Lithuanian literature began in the 16th century with the publication of the first printed book, *Katekizmo prasti žodžiai*.
The 16th and 17th centuries focused on religious literature, with key contributors including authors like Donelaitis, whose poem *Metai* is significant in Lithuanian fiction.
The 19th century saw movements like the Book smugglers, vital for the survival of the Lithuanian language and literature during Tsarist annexation.
Prominent 20th-century authors include significant figures in modern Lithuanian literature, while the 21st century introduced new authors like Renata Šerelytė.
Lithuanian architecture features Baroque and Art Deco styles, with significant historical sites including castles and an open-air museum preserving traditional architecture.
The largest museum of art conservation and display in Lithuania was founded in 1933.
Key artistic institutions include the National Gallery of Art, museums dedicated to archaeology and modern art.
Lithuania has notable theatres, with prominent theatre directors and festivals, including Theatre of Freaks and Sirenos.
Early Lithuanian cinema began in the late 19th century, with influential filmmakers and significant contributions in the early 20th century.
The country has a rich cultural heritage reflected in its museums, theatre, and cinema, showcasing both history and contemporary works.
4,265,414 cinema tickets were sold in Lithuania in 2018.
Lithuanian folk music has roots in both stringed and wind instrument cultures, often used for rituals.
Folk song genres include Multipart Songs, Wedding Songs, and Ritual Songs.
Mikalojus Konstantinas Čiurlionis contributed significantly to Lithuanian culture through his music and art and is considered a pioneer of abstract art in Europe.
The Dainų šventė festival, started in 1924, gathers around 30,000 singers and dancers in Lithuania every four years.
Active jazz scene in Lithuania during Soviet occupation, with the establishment of Vilnius Jazz School by Ganelin/Tarasov/Chekasin trio in 1970-71.
Emergence of rock music in Lithuania from 1965 as a form of protest against the Soviet regime, with notable bands like Kertukai and Aitvarai.
Usage of metaphorical lyrics by bands like Antis to criticize the Soviet regime, exemplified in songs like 'Zombiai' and 'Kolorado vabalai'.
Foje band gained immense popularity in the early independence years and produced famous anthems like 'Trys milijonai' and 'Nebetyli sirgaliai'.
Lithuanian cuisine is influenced by its climate, featuring locally grown products and dairy specialties, with similarities to Northern European cuisines.
Dairy products are essential in Lithuanian cuisine, including cottage cheese, sour cream, and soured milk.
Traditional Lithuanian meat products are seasoned, matured, and smoked, featuring items like smoked sausages and ham.
Soups and porridges are common in daily meals, with varieties including cabbage soup and cold-beet soup.
Rye bread is a staple food, integral to family rituals and consumed at every meal.
Lithuania has a rich beer culture, being one of the top countries in Europe for beer consumption, with a thriving microbrewery scene.
Eight Lithuanian restaurants featured in White Guide Baltic Top 30.
The local restaurant guide will be published on June 13, 2024.
Lithuania respects freedom of expression but has limitations on hate speech and disinformation.
Top-selling newspapers and TV channels in Lithuania were noted in 2021 for their popularity.
Lithuania has two key state holidays honoring its historical statehood and independence.
An annual festival commemorates the anniversary of a significant person's death and attracts thousands of visitors and craftsmen.
Notable festivals include various national holidays and celebrations, particularly in Lithuania.
Key Lithuanian holidays include New Year's Day, Statehood Day, and Christmas, each with specific cultural significances.
Basketball is the most popular sport in Lithuania, with a strong national team and significant historical achievements in international competitions.
Lithuania has a proud basketball heritage with notable players and a fan base that actively supports the sport.
Lithuania has won a total of Olympic medals, including 6 gold in swimming.
The most successful Olympic athlete of independent Lithuania has multiple gold and bronze medals.
A 15-year-old swimmer won a gold medal at the London Olympics, increasing the sport's popularity.
Lithuania hosted its first FIFA tournament.
Few Lithuanian athletes have excelled in winter sports, despite facilities available in the country.

Lithuania declared restoration of independence from the Soviet Union on 11 March 1990.
The Lithuanian parliament affirmed the restoration of sovereign rights infringed upon since 1940.
The act of independence reasserted a prior declaration made over seventy years earlier.
The declaration was a culmination of Lithuania's efforts over two years to reclaim independence.
Lithuania's independence was initially annulled in 1940 due to a pact between Stalin and Hitler.
